name: Report a Telecom / Utility Outage and Get a Credit Applied
description: Call a telecom (cable, fiber, mobile) or utility provider AFTER an outage has been resolved to claim a service-interruption credit — proportional to outage duration, applied to the current or next bill, with a confirmation number.
disclaimer: Most providers do NOT auto-credit outages — you must request it. Credit policies vary: many telecoms credit pro-rata for any outage > 4 hours; some require ≥ 24 hours; some only credit if you call. Utilities may have a tariff-defined credit (e.g. $25 per 12 hours over a threshold) and a more generous regulatory option after a major event.

context.when_to_use: Service has been restored AND the outage either lasted long enough to qualify per the provider's policy/tariff OR caused tangible harm (spoiled food in fridge, lost work time, missed appointments). Best within 30 days of the outage; some providers cap at 60 days.
preconditions: Approximate outage start and end times (within 1 hour is fine).
Original ticket / case number from when the outage was reported.
If claiming consequential damage (spoiled food, generator fuel): receipts/photos.
Knowledge of whether the outage was on the provider's published outage map / Twitter feed (third-party proof helps if account-level records are missing).

principles: Lead with the FACT of the outage and the ASK for credit in the same sentence. Reps process credit requests as a one-step transaction when framed cleanly.
Cite the original ticket number. With a ticket, the rep can pull the duration directly; without one, they may insist they 'have no record' and refuse.
Know the policy or tariff. 'Your service agreement provides a prorated credit for outages exceeding 24 hours' lands very differently from 'I think I'm owed something'.
Recognise wrong-queue early. Outage credits are typically billing or 'customer care', NOT tech support. If you're talking to a tech, you'll get a 'we'll note it' instead of an actual credit.

Consequential damage (food spoilage during a multi-day power outage, hotel during prolonged outage) is sometimes claimable, but usually goes through a separate claims/insurance process — not the standard credit line. Don't conflate them on the same call.

phrases.opener: Hi, account [number]. I had a service outage from approximately [start] to [end] — that's [duration]. Ticket number [number]. I'd like to request a service-interruption credit applied to my next bill.
phrases.no_ticket_opener: Hi, account [number]. I had a service outage from [start] to [end]. I don't have a ticket number — your outage map / status page confirmed it at the time. I'd like to request a service-interruption credit.
phrases.queue_check: Are you the team that processes outage credits? I want to make sure I'm not in tech support — I'm not troubleshooting, the service is back.
phrases.transfer_request: Could you do a warm transfer to billing / outage credits and stay on the line until they pick up? I'd rather not lose my hold position.
phrases.cite_policy: My understanding of your service agreement is that outages over [threshold] qualify for a prorated credit. Can you confirm the policy and calculate what applies to my [duration] outage?
phrases.ask_pro_rata: What's the per-day or per-hour credit rate? I want to confirm the math before we hang up — daily rate is roughly $[monthly / 30], and outage was [duration].
phrases.ask_application: Will this credit appear on my next bill, or as an immediate adjustment to the current balance? And how is it labelled on the bill so I can verify?
phrases.consequential_damage: Separately — the outage caused [spoiled food / hotel cost / lost work]. Is there a claims process for consequential damages, separate from the service credit?
phrases.supervisor_request: Thanks for digging through all that. Could you loop in a supervisor? The credit you're offering doesn't match my read of the policy, and I want to be sure before I close this out.
phrases.lock_in_writing: Please email me: credit amount, application date, line item label, and confirmation number. I want it in writing.
phrases.graceful_close: Thanks — $[amount] credit, applied on [bill date], confirmation [number]. I'll watch for the email.

tactics.name: Open with the ask
tactics.script: Use `opener` (with ticket) or `no_ticket_opener` (without). Mention duration in hours so the rep can map it to policy thresholds immediately.

tactics.name: Recognise wrong-queue / tech support trap
tactics.when: Rep starts running diagnostics or asks 'is the service back now?'.
tactics.script: Use `queue_check` and `transfer_request`. Tech support's job is to fix; billing's job is to credit. Different metrics, different scripts.

tactics.name: Cite the policy / tariff
tactics.when: Rep hedges on whether a credit applies.
tactics.script: Use `cite_policy`. Even a vague 'my understanding is over 24 hours qualifies' often triggers the rep to look up the actual policy.

tactics.name: Verify the math
tactics.script: Use `ask_pro_rata`. Reps sometimes offer a token $5 'goodwill' credit when a prorated tariff would mandate $25-$40. Doing the math live keeps them honest.

tactics.name: Confirm application
tactics.script: Use `ask_application`. Some credits go to the current bill (visible quickly), some to the next bill. Knowing which prevents a 'where's my credit' follow-up.

tactics.name: Separate consequential damage as its own track
tactics.when: User had spoiled food, hotel costs, etc.
tactics.script: Use `consequential_damage`. Do NOT try to roll it into the service credit — they're different processes. Most providers have a small-claims / damage form.

tactics.name: Escalate if the math is wrong
tactics.when: Rep offers a credit smaller than policy implies.
tactics.script: Use `supervisor_request`. Frame the supervisor ask as policy-clarification, not complaint — that gets the warm handoff.

boundaries: Do NOT fabricate or exaggerate outage duration. Providers can pull network logs; an exaggerated claim disqualifies the request entirely.
Do NOT request a credit for a brief outage (under the policy threshold) — it burns goodwill for the next real outage.
Do NOT accept a 'goodwill credit' that's smaller than the tariff-defined amount without asking for the formula.
Do NOT skip the consequential-damage claim form if real damage occurred — service credit alone won't cover it.
success_signals: Credit amount calculated against the actual policy / tariff.
Application date and line item confirmed.
If applicable: consequential-damage claim form sent separately.
failure_signals: Rep insists 'no record' of the outage and refuses to look up the ticket.
Offered credit is materially smaller than tariff implies and supervisor declines to escalate.
Credit is offered as 'a one-time goodwill' rather than as a service credit (loses precedent for future outages).

exit_strategy.on_success: Confirm amount, application date, confirmation number. Watch the next 1-2 bills to verify the credit appeared.
exit_strategy.on_failure: Escalate via the provider's executive customer-relations email (publicly listed). For utilities, file with the state Public Utility Commission citing the tariff. For telecom, file with the FCC for service-quality issues exceeding 24 hours.
follow_ups: Next bill: verify credit applied and labelled correctly.
If consequential damage was claimed: submit the claim form within the provider's window (usually 30-60 days).
If a major regional event (storm, wildfire): watch for an automatic class-wide credit announced by the provider — request it separately if your account didn't auto-receive it.

required_user_info: Account number and account holder name
Outage start and end times (approximate is fine)
Original ticket / case number if available
